topics.title: Big-O Complexity Analysis
topics.description: Big-O notation describes how an algorithm's time or space requirements grow as input size n grows, ignoring constants and lower-order terms. The hierarchy from best to worst: O(1) → O(log n) → O(n) → O(n log n) → O(n²) → O(2ⁿ) → O(n!). Amortized analysis averages the cost across a sequence of operations — ArrayList.add() is O(1) amortized despite occasional O(n) resize. Space complexity counts memory used beyond the input.
topics.story: You're a detective. The suspect list is n people. Checking one specific suspect = O(1) (you know the room number). Binary searching a sorted suspect list = O(log n) (halve each time). Reading all police reports = O(n). Sorting the suspects = O(n log n). Checking all pairs for alibi conflicts = O(n²). Each subset of suspects = O(2ⁿ). Your boss doesn't care about exact time — just 'will this be done before retirement?'. Big-O is exactly that: 'as the city grows (n increases), how does my workload scale?'
topics.analogy: O(1) = looking up a word with its page number (direct index). O(log n) = binary search in a dictionary (halve the pages each step). O(n) = reading the entire dictionary once. O(n log n) = sorting a deck of n cards with merge sort. O(n²) = comparing every card to every other card. O(2ⁿ) = generating all possible teams from n players. Drop constants: O(3n) = O(n). Drop lower terms: O(n² + n) = O(n²). Always analyze the dominant term.
topics.memoryAnchor: O(1): array index, HashMap get. O(log n): binary search, BST ops, divide-and-conquer. O(n): linear scan, single loop. O(n log n): merge sort, heap sort, most 'good' sorting. O(n²): nested loops, bubble/selection sort. O(2ⁿ): subsets, brute-force recursion. O(n!): permutations. Space: O(1) iterative, O(n) recursive stack, O(n) extra array. Amortized: ArrayList add is O(1) amortized (doubles on resize). Master theorem: T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n) → classify recurrence.
topics.firstPrinciples: Count the dominant operation as a function of n. Single loop over n = O(n). Nested loop = O(n²). Each level of recursion halving n = O(log n). Recursion making two calls on half the input = O(n log n). Constants don't change growth rate — O(100n) and O(n) have the same asymptotic behavior. Big-O is an upper bound; Big-Ω is lower bound; Big-Θ is tight bound.
topics.bruteForce: Without complexity analysis: write whatever code solves the problem, submit, hope it passes. Works for small inputs, TLEs on large inputs. Common mistake: O(n²) solution that works on test cases (n=100) but fails on judge (n=10⁶).
topics.optimizedApproach: Analyze before coding: 'n can be 10⁶ → O(n²) = 10¹² ops → TLE. Need O(n log n) or O(n)'. Use hash maps to eliminate inner loops (O(n²) → O(n)). Use sorting + binary search (O(n²) → O(n log n)). Use sliding window (O(n²) → O(n)). Use prefix sums (O(n²) range sum → O(1) per query).
topics.whenToUse: Always estimate complexity before starting. For n≤10⁶: O(n) or O(n log n). For n≤10⁴: O(n²) acceptable. For n≤20: O(2ⁿ) acceptable. For n≤12: O(n!) acceptable. Space: O(1) when asked 'in-place', O(n) auxiliary usually fine.

examples.title: Identifying Complexity of Common Patterns
examples.explanation: The key insight: nested loops usually mean O(n²); one loop = O(n); halving = O(log n). Always ask 'can I replace the inner loop with a HashSet lookup (O(1))?' — this transforms O(n²) to O(n). Amortized analysis: ArrayList doubles on resize, so although individual add can be O(n), averaged over n operations each is O(1).
examples.realWorldUse: Interview signal: state complexity before and after optimization. 'I see a O(n²) solution using nested loops. I can optimize to O(n) using a HashMap to store complements.' Database: full table scan O(n) vs index lookup O(log n). Sorting: always O(n log n) lower bound for comparison-based sorts. Counting sort O(n+k) when range k is small.

examples.title: Recurrence Relations — Merge Sort, Binary Search
examples.explanation: The recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) solves to O(n log n) by the Master Theorem. Visualizing the recursion tree: log n levels, each level does O(n) total work. Binary search's T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) has log n levels with O(1) work each = O(log n). The mid = lo + (hi-lo)/2 form prevents integer overflow for large arrays.
examples.realWorldUse: Interview: always state recurrence for recursive solutions. Binary search template reused for: find first/last occurrence, find insertion point, search in rotated array. Merge sort stable sort — used when stability required. TimSort (Java's Arrays.sort for objects) uses merge sort + insertion sort hybrid.

problems.title: Determine Complexity of Code Snippets
problems.hint3: Two independent loops: O(n) + O(m) = O(n+m).
problems.testCases:
1. O(n²)
2. O(log n)
3. O(n log n)
4. O(n+m)
5. O(n)

problems.title: Two Sum — O(n²) vs O(n)
problems.description:
Compare two implementations of Two Sum: O(n^2) brute force vs O(n) HashMap.

**Example:**
twoSumBrute([2,7,11,15], 9) => [0,1] — O(n^2): check all pairs
twoSumHash([2,7,11,15], 9) => [0,1] — O(n): HashMap lookup

Benchmark both for n=10000:
brute: ~100ms, hash: ~1ms

Return both results and execution times.
problems.hint1: Brute: nested loop checking all pairs.
problems.hint2: Optimized: for each num, check if (target - num) is in HashMap.
problems.hint3: HashMap stores num → index. One pass: check then put.

problems.title: Count Operations and Predict Runtime
problems.description:
Count the number of operations in each algorithm and predict which is faster for n=1000:

1. `bubbleSort(arr)` — O(n^2) worst case
2. `mergeSort(arr)` — O(n log n)
3. `binarySearch(sorted, target)` — O(log n)

**Example:**
countOps("bubbleSort", 1000) => ~500000
countOps("mergeSort", 1000) => ~10000
countOps("binarySearch", 1000) => ~10

Return `{name: estimated_ops}`
problems.hint3: Measure with System.nanoTime() inside both loops.
